mcqs.question: In Weighted Interval Scheduling, what does p(i) represent?

options.text: The interval with the highest weight before i
The rightmost interval that finishes before interval i starts
The interval with the maximum profit among compatible intervals
The first interval in the sorted order

mcqs.explanation: p(i) is defined as the index of the rightmost interval that finishes before or at the start time of interval i. It helps determine compatible intervals for the DP solution.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the DP solution for Weighted Interval Scheduling?
mcqs.explanation: O(n log n) comes from sorting intervals O(n log n), computing p(i) using binary search O(n log n), and DP computation O(n).

mcqs.question: What is the recurrence relation for DP[i] in Weighted Interval Scheduling?
mcqs.explanation: The recurrence chooses between two options: either exclude interval i (DP[i-1]) or include it (weight[i] + DP[p(i)]), taking the maximum.

mcqs.question: Why does the greedy approach by earliest finish time fail for Weighted Interval Scheduling?

options.text: Because it doesn't consider weights at all
Because it may select an interval with low weight even when higher weight intervals are available
Because sorting by finish time is incorrect

mcqs.explanation: The greedy choice of picking the earliest finishing interval may not maximize total weight because weights vary. A later finishing interval might have significantly higher weight.

mcqs.question: What is the base case for Weighted Interval Scheduling DP?
mcqs.explanation: DP[0] = 0 represents that with no intervals considered, the maximum weight achievable is zero.

mcqs.question: Which data structure is used to find p(i) efficiently?
mcqs.explanation: Binary search is used on the sorted finish times to find p(i), the predecessor of interval i, in O(log n) time.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of the Weighted Interval Scheduling DP solution?
mcqs.explanation: We maintain a DP array of size n+1, where each entry stores the maximum weight for that prefix of intervals.

mcqs.question: In Weighted Interval Scheduling, intervals must be sorted by what?
mcqs.explanation: Intervals must be sorted by finish time to ensure that p(i) can be computed correctly using binary search on finish times.

mcqs.question: What is the key property that makes dynamic programming applicable to WISP?
mcqs.explanation: Optimal substructure means the optimal solution can be built from optimal solutions of subproblems, which is essential for DP.

mcqs.question: If p(i) = 0, what does it mean?

options.text: Interval i has no compatible predecessor
Interval i is the first interval
Interval i overlaps with all previous intervals
The weight of interval i is zero

mcqs.explanation: When p(i) = 0, it means no interval finishes before interval i starts, so there is no compatible previous interval to consider.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT an application of Weighted Interval Scheduling?

options.text: Meeting room scheduling with different profits
Sorting an array in ascending order

mcqs.explanation: Sorting is not an application of WISP. WISP is used for scheduling problems where intervals have weights and cannot overlap.

mcqs.question: How do you reconstruct the selected intervals in WISP?

options.text: Start from the first interval and move forward
Use binary search on the DP array
Backtrack from DP[n] using the recurrence

mcqs.explanation: Starting from DP[n], backtrack: if weight[i] + DP[p(i)] > DP[i-1], include interval i and move to p(i); otherwise, move to i-1.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between unweighted and weighted interval scheduling?

options.text: Unweighted uses greedy, weighted uses DP always
Unweighted maximizes count, weighted maximizes sum of weights

mcqs.explanation: Unweighted interval scheduling maximizes the number of non-overlapping intervals, while weighted maximizes the total weight/profit.

mcqs.question: What precomputation is needed before applying the DP formula?

options.text: Compute the weight sum of all intervals
Find p(i) for each interval using binary search

mcqs.explanation: Before applying DP, we need to compute p(i) for each interval by finding the rightmost compatible interval using binary search on sorted finish times.

mcqs.question: Which technique is used to compute p(i) for all intervals efficiently?

options.text: Binary search on sorted finish times for each interval

mcqs.explanation: For each interval i, we use binary search on the sorted finish times array to find p(i), giving O(n log n) total time.

mcqs.question: The Weighted Interval Scheduling problem exhibits which property?
mcqs.explanation: WISP exhibits both optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming solution.

mcqs.question: In the DP array, DP[i] represents what?

options.text: Maximum weight considering intervals 1 to i
Minimum weight considering intervals 1 to i
Number of intervals selected up to i

mcqs.explanation: DP[i] stores the maximum total weight achievable by considering intervals from 1 to i in the sorted order.

mcqs.question: What is the result of sorting intervals by start time instead of finish time?

options.text: May produce incorrect solution or make p(i) computation difficult

mcqs.explanation: Sorting by start time breaks the property needed for binary search to find p(i), potentially leading to incorrect results or requiring a different approach.

mcqs.question: Can Weighted Interval Scheduling be solved using memoization?

options.text: Yes, with same time complexity O(n log n)

mcqs.explanation: Top-down memoization can solve WISP. The time complexity remains O(n log n) as each subproblem is computed once and p(i) is found using binary search.

mcqs.question: Two intervals are compatible if they do what?
mcqs.explanation: Two intervals are compatible if they don't overlap; typically, one must finish before or at the start of the other begins.
